articles.title: New international student permit approvals for Canada fell below COVID levels in 2025
articles.summary: Canada approved 75,372 new study permits for international students in 2025. This figure represents a 64% year-over-year decline and an 18% decrease from the 2020 pandemic low.
articles.insight:
Canada’s sharp 64% YoY drop in new study permits—falling even below 2020 pandemic lows—may reflect tightened immigration policies, reduced post-study work incentives, or shifting perceptions among key source markets, though it’s unclear how much this signals broader global demand shifts versus Canada-specific factors.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application trends and sentiment data from overlapping source regions (e.g., India, Nigeria, Philippines), while reviewing their own visa support services and post-graduation outcome messaging to ensure competitiveness amid evolving student priorities.

articles.title: Number of Hong Kong school leavers seeking education elsewhere hits 14-year low
articles.summary: The number of Hong Kong secondary school leavers pursuing higher education outside the city dropped to 2,671 in 2025, representing 6.5% of the 40,948 Form Six graduates enrolled in full-time courses, marking a 13% decline from 2024’s figure of 3,061 and the lowest level in 14 years. An expert cited increased competition from mainland Chinese students applying to local universities as a key factor behind this trend.
articles.insight:
This decline in Hong Kong students studying abroad may reflect shifting preferences toward mainland Chinese universities, tighter overseas visa policies, or improved local higher education options—though it’s unclear how much this signals broader regional demand changes versus Hong Kong-specific factors.  
Admissions teams should monitor application trends from Hong Kong across undergraduate and postgraduate levels, track policy shifts in China’s cross-border education approvals, and assess whether mainland competition is reshaping Hong Kong students’ perceptions of value and risk in studying overseas.

articles.title: UK: 7 in 10 universities report declining international postgraduate enrolments; visa rejections are part of the story
articles.summary: A recent survey by the British Universities International Liaison Association (BUILA) found that 70% of UK universities reported a decline in international postgraduate enrolments in January 2026 compared with January 2025, with an average reduction of 31%. The drop was particularly pronounced among students from “high-risk” markets, and visa rejections were cited as a contributing factor.
articles.insight:
This UK enrolment decline—driven partly by visa rejections and concentrated in high-risk markets—may signal growing sensitivity among international postgraduates to perceived immigration barriers, though it’s unclear whether this reflects broader demand shifts or UK-specific policy impacts.  
Admissions teams should track visa refusal rates, source-market sentiment surveys, and competitor enrolment trends in key regions—noting that similar policy tightening elsewhere could amplify spillover effects on student decision-making.

articles.title: Pause on Some OPT Applications Has Left Students in Limbo for Months
articles.summary: A pause on certain Optional Practical Training (OPT) applications has left international students in limbo for months, according to a report published on April 27, 2026. Students from the 40 countries and territories subject to the Trump administration’s travel ban may miss their opportunity to work in the U.S. as the spring semester concludes.
articles.insight:
This news signals potential erosion in U.S. appeal for students from affected countries, as prolonged OPT uncertainty may dampen demand—though broader trends in global mobility, institutional reputation, and alternative destinations will moderate the impact.  
Admissions teams should monitor application volumes and yield rates from impacted regions, while reviewing messaging around post-study work pathways to ensure clarity and responsiveness to evolving visa realities.

articles.title: Chinese international students gradually leave America
articles.summary: For the first time in over ten years, the United States is no longer among the top two destinations for Chinese international students. This shift reflects a growing emphasis on cost and career prospects rather than prestige.
articles.insight:
This news signals a potential recalibration in Chinese student preferences, where affordability and post-study employment outcomes may now outweigh traditional prestige factors—though it remains unclear whether this reflects a structural shift or short-term volatility driven by geopolitical, economic, or policy variables.  
Admissions teams should monitor application volume trends by cohort, scholarship uptake rates, and destination-specific visa approval timelines from China, while also benchmarking career support offerings and cost-of-living transparency against key competitors like the UK and Australia.
